equations.name: Gibbs free energy

node_breakdown.note: T and ΔS are free parameters

equations.notes: Minimal linear form. With T33, sub(x,y) costs 2 nodes as listed in the routing table.

equations.name: Standard Gibbs from equilibrium constant

node_breakdown.note: R is a constant (free), T is a free parameter

equations.notes: R is a scalar constant and is free; mul(R,T) still costs 2 because T is a variable. neg is required to represent the minus sign — cannot be absorbed into a constant here since it negates the entire product.

equations.name: Gibbs with reaction quotient
equations.notes: One node more than Eq 2 because addition costs 3 vs negation's 2. ΔG° treated as a free parameter (known constant in a given context).

equations.name: van't Hoff equation (integrated form)

node_breakdown_rhs.note: 1/R is a numeric constant (free); mul of ΔH° by that constant costs 2

equations.notes: 1/R is a numeric constant (free terminal). RHS alone: 12 nodes. If the LHS ln(K₂/K₁) is also treated as a computed expression (not a directly observable primitive), add 5 nodes for a full-equation cost of 17. Conventional analysis counts RHS only = 12.

equations.name: Clausius-Clapeyron equation
equations.notes: Structurally identical to van't Hoff integrated form (Eq 4). Same tree topology, different physical parameters (ΔHvap vs ΔH°, P vs K). Full-equation cost is also 17 if LHS ln(P₂/P₁) is counted.

equations.name: Entropy of mixing (2-component)

node_breakdown.note: R is a constant (free); mul still costs 2

equations.notes: The xᵢ·ln(xᵢ) pattern repeats twice, one per component. The add node (cost 3) is the most expensive single operation. An n-component generalization would add 3 + 2*ln + 2*mul per additional component (plus add nodes to chain the sum).

equations.name: Arrhenius in terms of Gibbs (Form 1)

node_breakdown.note: A is a free parameter (pre-exponential factor)

equations.notes: Single exp node. ΔG‡ is treated as a single free parameter (activation Gibbs energy).

equations.name: Arrhenius in terms of Gibbs (Form 2)
equations.notes: Two exp nodes vs one in Form 1. Splitting ΔG‡ = ΔH‡ − TΔS‡ adds 5 nodes (second exp + extra mul for ΔS‡/R + two outer muls). Form 2 costs 16 vs Form 1 costs 11 — a difference of 5 nodes for the thermodynamic decomposition.

cross_equation_notes.eq4_eq5_isomorphism: van't Hoff (Eq 4) and Clausius-Clapeyron (Eq 5) are structurally identical — same tree topology, same node count (RHS: 12, full: 17). They differ only in the physical domain of parameters.
cross_equation_notes.eq7_form_comparison: Arrhenius Form 2 costs 5 more nodes than Form 1 (16 vs 11). The extra cost comes from: a second exp node (+1), mul(ΔS‡, 1/R) (+2), and two additional outer mul nodes to chain the products (+2).
cross_equation_notes.lhs_treatment: For equations where the LHS is itself a non-trivial expression (ln(K₂/K₁), ln(P₂/P₁)), the conventional SuperBEST analysis counts the RHS expression tree only. The full-equation cost (LHS + RHS) is provided separately for completeness.
cross_equation_notes.constant_absorption: Scalar physical constants (R, and 1/R as its reciprocal) are treated as free terminals. mul(parameter, constant) still costs 2 because one operand is a variable.
